coredata.dc:title: Universal role of oxygen in full-visible-region photoluminescence of diamond nanocrystals
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenxia
Niu, Xianghong
Chen, Xifang
Guo, Xiaoxiao
Wang, Jinlan
Fan, Jiyang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanocrystals (NCs) exhibit very intricate luminescence and the underlying mechanism remains a mystery and highly debated. Herein we study the luminescence properties of the wet chemistry-derived diamond NCs and observe peculiar phenomenon of concurrent multiband luminescence across the whole visible region in them. The surface structural and luminescence investigation in conjunction with density functional theory calculation reveal fruitful oxygen-related surface defects in these diamond NCs, and such defects create respective energy levels in the band gap leading to observed luminescences. The calculation shows that these surface traps have either localized or delocalized molecular orbitals, and significant spatial overlap between the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals of amide-terminated diamond NCs leads to extremely large radiative decay rate as well as unprecedented high quantum yield of 50.4%, in strong contrast with usually low quantum yield of 2.5–5.0% in oxygen-terminated diamond NCs. The result indicates these surface defects are popular and they play important roles in luminescence of the large family of various (graphite, diamond, and C8) carbon as well as SiC and C3N4 NCs.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface structure properties of diamond nanocrystals
Theoretical study of surface defects of diamond nanocrystals
Energy level distribution of surface defects

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (c) selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the G-DNCs. (b) TEM image and (d) SAED pattern of the A-DNCs. The insets show high-resolution TEM images of two typical particles. The rings in (c) are fit of measured diffraction spots. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL spectra of G-DNC aqueous solutions: (top) as-prepared and (bottom) N2H4·H2O-passivated. (b) PL spectra of G-DNC aqueous solutions: (top) KOH-treated and (bottom) KOH-treated followed by NaBH4 reduction. (c) PL spectra of A-DNC aqueous solutions: (top) as-prepared and (bottom) HCl-treated. (d) Stokes shift versus excitation wavelength for N2H4·H2O-passivated G-DNCs and HCl-treated A-DNCs. (e) PL intensity versus excitation wavelength at different emission wavelengths for as-prepared G-DNCs. (f) Time-resolved PL spectra of four diamond nanocrystal solutions. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
PL excitation spectra of (a, b) G-DNC aqueous solutions and (c) A-DNC aqueous solutions. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) FTIR spectra of G-DNCs and A-DNCs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) N 1s for as-prepared and N2H4·H2O-passivated G-DNCs as well as as-prepared and HCl-treated A-DNCs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(top) Six configurations of DNCs bonded with different surface functional groups used for DFT calculation. (middle and bottom) Corresponding isosurfaces of wave functions of lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Schematic of energy levels of various surface traps in the diamond nanocrystal that result in photoluminescence. Types I, II, III, and IV surface traps are separately related to COOH, C(O)NH2, COC, and C(O)O functional groups. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Universal role of oxygen in full-visible-region photoluminescence of diamond nanocrystals

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanocrystals (NCs) exhibit very intricate luminescence and the underlying mechanism remains a mystery and highly debated. Herein we study the luminescence properties of the wet chemistry-derived diamond NCs and observe peculiar phenomenon of concurrent multiband luminescence across the whole visible region in them. The surface structural and luminescence investigation in conjunction with density functional theory calculation reveal fruitful oxygen-related surface defects in these diamond NCs, and such defects create respective energy levels in the band gap leading to observed luminescences. The calculation shows that these surface traps have either localized or delocalized molecular orbitals, and significant spatial overlap between the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals of amide-terminated diamond NCs leads to extremely large radiative decay rate as well as unprecedented high quantum yield of 50.4%, in strong contrast with usually low quantum yield of 2.5–5.0% in oxygen-terminated diamond NCs. The result indicates these surface defects are popular and they play important roles in luminescence of the large family of various (graphite, diamond, and C8) carbon as well as SiC and C3N4 NCs.

ce:section.ce:para: The large family of carbon element-containing nanocrystals (NCs), including various carbon (graphite, diamond, C8), SiC, and C3N4 NCs, have attracted great interest in recent years due to their excellent physical and chemical properties [1–5]. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of these materials are all complicated, but they share some inexplicably similar PL spectral behaviors. They have wide applications in many areas such as bioimaging, catalysis, and drug delivery [6–10]. Among this large family, carbon NCs are paid more and more attention to during the past several years because they are superiorly-biocompatible luminescent material and they have the most cheap and abundant source materials on earth [11–14]. The origin of luminescence in carbon NCs remains a big mystery and highly debated, the proposed mechanisms vary from quantum confinement effect to various defects [15,16]. Some investigations suggest that the luminescence features exhibited in carbon NCs may result from oxygen- or nitrogen-containing functional groups [17,18]. Some studies show that the PL of the carbon NCs is pH dependent [19,20] or related to polarity of solvent [9]. While these viewpoints could explain partial optical properties of the carbon NCs, some mysteries still remain. For example, what are the specific surface traps responsible for the observed luminescence? Why can carbon NCs emit light in such a wide spectral region from infrared to near UV? Why does the PL shift considerably under some circumstances? What is the role of nitrogen in luminescence of carbon NCs? Herein, we shed light on these issues based on both experimental and theoretical studies through investigating the luminescence properties of the colloidal diamond-structured carbon NCs.

Diamond NCs (DNCs) are ideal prototype material for elucidating the common origin of luminescence in carbon-element-containing NCs. This is because diamond has a large band gap (5.5 eV), DNCs have higher energy gaps owing to quantum confinement effect [21,22] and thus they cannot emit visible light by interband transitions. Visible luminescence is usually realized in DNCs by intentionally creating fluorophores in them such as nitrogen-vacancy and silicon-vacancy defects [23–25], such luminescent DNCs have been widely used in quantum information and biological science [26]. Theoretical calculation reveals that the optical gap of DNCs is tuned towards the infrared spectral region by varying the number of surface CS double bonds [27]. Hence DNCs may emit visible/infrared light by tuning their surface chemical structures. Using glucose and ammonium citrate as two carbon source materials, here we develop a facile hydrothermal method to synthesize colloidal diamond nanocrystals with oxygen-rich or nitrogen-rich surface functional groups; they exhibit visible PL with a series of emission bands across the whole visible region. We focus on elucidating the underlying luminescence mechanism behind this enticing phenomenon via experimental study combining with theoretical calculation. Various strategies are employed to modify the surface structure of the DNCs to study their surface-structure dependence of luminescence, including N2H4·H2O (hydrazine hydrate) passivation, NaBH4 (sodium borohydride) reduction, and alkali or acid treatment. The experimental study in conjunction with time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) calculation elucidate the crucial role of various surface oxygen (nitrogen) defects in generating the luminescence of the DNCs.

Glucose, ammonium citrate, ammonium bicarbonate, hydrazine hydrate, hydrochloric acid, sodium borohydride, and KOH were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The DNCs were synthesized by using the hydrothermal method.

The first batch DNCS were synthesized with glucose (G-DNCs). The mixed solution of 3 g glucose, 10 mL HCl, and 20 mL deionized water underwent ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The mixed solution reacted at 140 °C for 2 h in a teflon lined stainless steel autoclave placed in a drying oven. The resultant solution underwent centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 30 min, and the supernatant containing DNCs was separated out and divided into three equal shares. One share served as the as-prepared sample. 3 mL hydrazine hydrate was introduced into the second share and then the mixed solution experienced hydrothermal treatment at 120 °C for 2 h. The pH of the third share was tuned to 12 by adding KOH and it was then divided into two shares. One is the reference sample. NaBH4 was added into another share and the mixed solution underwent hydrothermal reduction reaction at 80 °C for 6 h. The concentrations of the diamond nanocrystal solutions in each group were tuned to be identical through dilution.

The second batch DNCs were synthesized with ammonium citrate (A-DNCs). 3 g ammonium citrate and 1 g ammonium bicarbonate were co-dissolved in 20 mL deionized water and then underwent ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The mixed solution reacted at 180 °C for 10 h in the autoclave. The resultant solution underwent centrifugation, and the supernatant containing DNCs (pH = 8) was separated out and divided into ten equal shares. One acts as the as-prepared sample. The pH of another three shares is separately tuned to 9, 10, and 13 by adding KOH, the pH of the other six shares is separately tuned to 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7 by adding HCl. The luminescence properties of these ten diamond nanocrystal solutions were comparatively studied. A fraction of the solution with pH 3 was further divided into two shares and the pH of one share was tuned back to 8 by adding KOH. The luminescence properties of these two shares and the pristine diamond nanocrystal solution (pH = 8) were comparatively studied. The concentrations of the diamond nanocrystal solutions in each group were tuned to be identical through dilution.

The PL spectra were measured on a Fluorolog 3-TCSPC spectrofluorometer (HORIBA JOBIN YVON) with a Xe lamp as the light source. The UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy was performed on a Hitachi U-3900 UV/Vis spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was conducted on a Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrophotometer. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on a JEM-200CX TEM operating at 200 kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a PHI 5000 VersaProbe spectrometer equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα source. The quantum yield of the DNCs was measured by using 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution (quantum yield 54%) as the reference sample. The mass spectra were measured using a MSQ Plus mass spectrometer (Thermo).

The DNCs synthesized with glucose and ammonium citrate are separately called G-DNCs and A-DNCs. Fig. 1 a and b show their TEM images. The synthesized nanoparticles are approximately spherical. The G-DNCs have sizes between 2 and 10 nm with a most probable size of 6.3 nm (Fig. S1a); the size of the A-DNCs ranges from 2 to 5 nm with a most probable size of 3.3 nm (Fig. S1b). The lattice fringes of the nanoparticles (insets of Fig. 1a and b) are clear, indicating they are single crystals. The lattice spacing is 0.205 nm, being consistent with that of the (111) planes of cubic diamond [28]. Fig. 1c and d show the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of the nanoparticles, and the calculated lattice spacing values (Table S1) are well consistent with those of diamond.

The DNCs exhibit intriguing PL properties (Fig. 2 a–c). The PL spectra of the as-prepared G-DNCs comprise five distinct emission bands: two violet bands centered at 390 and 433 nm, a blue band at around 470 nm, a green band at 527 nm, and a red band at 614 nm. Both N2H4·H2O-passivation and KOH-treatment significantly reduce the intensity of the emission bands at 390, 433, 527, and 614 nm. The 470-nm emission band diminishes a little after passivation, whereas it keeps nearly unchanged after KOH treatment. After NaBH4 reduction, the 470-nm emission band becomes much weaker. Being quite different from the case of the G-DNCs, the PL of the A-DNCs has a single strong emission band at around 447 nm, whose peak position keeps unchanged as the excitation wavelength varies. Surprisingly, after HCl treatment, this emission band nearly vanishes, meanwhile the previously suppressed emission band at 435 nm becomes evident.

The PL excitation (PLE) spectra of the as-prepared G-DNCs comprise five peaks centered at around 310, 320, 368, 410, and 460 nm (Fig. 3 a). After N2H4·H2O passivation, the 368-nm excitation peak diminishes slightly, whereas all other four excitation peaks nearly disappear. The KOH treatment results in similar variation of the PLE spectra (Fig. 3b). After NaBH4-reduction of the KOH-treated G-DNCs, the intensity of the 368-nm peak drops substantially. Comparing the variation laws of the emission and excitation bands as the surface of the DNCs is changed, and considering the correspondence between the photon energy of PL peak and that of PLE peak, we conclude that the excitation bands at 310, 320, 368, 410, and 460 nm correspond separately to the emission bands at 390, 433, 470, 527, and 614 nm. The PLE spectra of the A-DNCs (Fig. 3c) show quite different features. They comprise a very strong peak at around 360 nm and a weak shoulder at around 325 nm. After HCl treatment, the 360-nm peak decreases largely and meantime the 325-nm peak becomes evident. Comparison with the variation of the PL peaks suggests that the 360-nm absorption band corresponds to the 447-nm emission band and the 325 (435)-nm excitation (emission) band has the same origin to that of the 320 (433)-nm excitation (emission) band observed in the G-DNCs. The remarkable variation of the six excitation (emission) bands of the DNCs after appropriate surface treatments points out that they all stem from surface defects rather than from intrinsic interband transition.

The strong PL of the A-DNCs indicates that amide termination of the surface of the DNCs is quite different from the usual oxygen termination. The quantum yields of the G-DNCs are below 5% (Table S2), close to that of the graphite CNCs and the C8 CNCs [29,30]. In contrast, the as-prepared A-DNCs have a high quantum yield 50.4%, being an order of magnitude larger than that of all carbon (graphite, C8, diamond) NCs reported in the literature. After HCL treatment, the quantum yield of the A-DNCs decreases to a low value close to that of the G-DNCs. This is because such surface treatment strongly diminishes the 447-nm emission band leaving only the bluer emission band that share the same origin to the 433-nm emission band of the G-DNCs. Fig. 2d shows the Stokes shift versus excitation wavelength for the N2H4·H2O-passivated G-DNCs and the HCl-treated A-DNCs. Clearly, two samples possess similar variation trends in Zones I and III, implying identical luminescence origin herein. However, the variation trends in Zone II are different. In this zone, the 470-nm emission had dominant contribution to the PL of the passivated G-DNCs; the PL spectrum of the HCl-treated A-DNCs contains partial contribution of the 447-nm emission band. Hence the different variation laws suggest different origins for these two emission bands. The Stokes shift curve for the passivated G-DNCs comprises three distinct regions: 310–320 nm, 340–380 nm, and 400–460 nm. This implies that the emission bands at 390 and 433 nm originate from the same type of surface-defect, the stable 470-nm emission band arises from a second-type surface defect, and the emission bands at 527 and 614 nm stem from another type of surface defect. Fig. 2e shows peak intensity versus excitation wavelength for each emission band of the as-prepared G-DNCs. The curve for the 390-nm emission band acquires a maximum at around 310 nm, being consistent with the first PLE peak. The curve for the 433-nm emission band is flat as it contains contribution from adjacent emission bands. Its maximum lies at around 320 nm, being the position of the second PLE peak. The curve for the 470-nm emission band is wider because it shifts somewhat as the excitation wavelength varies. Its maximum lies at around 368 nm, just the position of the third PLE peak. The curve for the 527-nm emission band has two maxima, one is contributed by the adjacent 470-nm emission band, and the other redder peak corresponds to the 410-nm PLE peak. The curve for the 614-nm emission band has two maxima, one is attributed to the contribution of the 527-nm emission band, and another redder one at around 460 nm is consistent with the fifth PLE peak. Hence the information extracted from Fig. 2e strongly supports our deduction that the excitation bands at 310, 320, 368, 410, and 460 nm correspond respectively to the emission bands at 390, 433, 470, 527, and 614 nm.

The time-resolved PL spectra (Fig. 2f) give further information concerning the origins of the multiple excitation/emission bands. Each decay curve is well fitted by using a double or triple exponential function
I
( t )
= a +
b 1
e
− t /
τ 1
+
b 2
e
− t /
τ 2
+
b 3
e
− t /
τ 3
, where a denotes the background signal, τ i and b i (i = 1, 2, 3) are lifetime and weight factor for each component. The relative amplitude of each component is derived by using
b i
τ i
/
∑ j
b j
τ j
. These values are displayed in Table S2. For the as-prepared G-DNCs and N2H4·H2O-passivated G-DNCs, the stable component with a large proportion and a lifetime of 2.8/3.0 ns is assigned to the 470-nm emission band, the 5.5-ns component contains contributions from the other four emission bands. After passivation, the lower-proportion component shows a lifetime of 13.5ns. For the A-DNCs, the big difference in relative amplitude indicates that the 5.3/3.6 and 6.7/8.8 ns components correspond to the 435 and 447-nm emission bands, respectively. The component with a small time constant of 0.5–0.8 ns is the instrumental response. The average lifetime (τ a) for each sample is calculated by using
∑ i
b i
τ i 2
/
(
∑ j
b j
τ j
)
. Using the values of quantum yield (Q) and average lifetime, the radiative decay rate k
r and nonradiative decay rate k
nr are calculated by using
Q =
k r
/
(
k r
+
k
n r
)
and
τ a
=
1 /
(
k r
+
k
n r
)
. The calculation shows (Table S2) that for the as-prepared A-DNCs, the radiative decay rate and nonradiative decay rate are roughly equal, whereas for the other three samples, the radiative decay rate is much smaller than the nonradiative decay rate. This confirms that the 447-nm emission band in the A-DNCs does have a special origin compared with the other emission bands in the A-DNCs and D-DNCs. Table S3 shows results of more time-resolved PL spectra of the as-prepared and treated G-DNCs and A-DNCs under different excitations along with the lifetime and relative amplitude of the fitted components. The probable assignment of the various components to the fluorescent surface defects is also shown. However, it should be noted that because usually several (up to five) types of surface defects contribute simultaneously to one time-resolved PL spectrum, as a result, each light emission at a specified wavelength possesses contributions from different surface defects, thus the direct correspondence between one fitted component and a specific surface defect is not so reliable.
Note that the luminescence lifetimes of the DNCs lie in the nanosecond region, suggesting they are appropriate candidates for biological imaging and optoelectronic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface structure properties of diamond nanocrystals
ce:section.ce:para: Why does each pair of emission/excitation bands behave distinctively when the surface of the DNCs is altered by using various methods? The remarkable change in light absorption and emission is subjected to arise from variation of the surface defect that produces such light emission and excitation. Therefore, we study how the surface structures of the DNCs change after various surface treatments by using infrared absorption spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 4 a displays the infrared absorption spectra. For the G-DNCs, both N2H4·H2O-passivation and KOH-treatment cause vanishing of the signal of the CO (in carboxylic acid group) stretching vibration (1720 cm−1). For the KOH-treated G-DNCs, the CO (in COOˉ) absorption signal arises at 1600 cm−1 [31], suggesting that COOH turns into COO− owing to acid‒alkali neutralization reaction. This variation is consistent with the variation of the four emission bands at 390, 433, 527, and 614 nm, suggesting that the CO-related functional groups act as surface traps leading to these light emissions. What is the origin of the 470-nm emission band? When the surface of the KOH-treated G-DNCs is reduced by NaBH4, the signal at 1058/1173 cm−1 ascribed to the COC stretching vibration diminishes significantly. This is consistent with weakening of the 470-nm emission band, suggesting that the COC functional group acts as the surface trap leading to the 470-nm light emission. In practice, it is well known that NaBH4 can reduce the epoxy group into the hydroxyl group while other species such as CC and COOH keep unchanged [32,33]. What is the origin of the strong 447-nm emission band? For the as-prepared A-DNCs, the characteristic absorption peaks of C(O)NH2 [amide group, abbreviated as C(O)NH2] are present in the infrared spectrum and they are: NH vibration at 3100–3500 cm−1 [31,34], CO vibration at 1630‒1690 cm−1 [35], NH vibration at 1500‒1650 cm−1 [35], and CN vibration at 1250‒1420 cm−1. The HCl treatment largely changes the surface structure of the A-DNCs: the C(O)NH2 signals nearly vanish, while the COOH signals become evident. This variation is consistent with the observed vanishing of the 447-nm emission band and becoming obvious of the 435-nm emission band. Hence the C(O)NH2 group acts as the surface trap leading to the strong light emission at around 447 nm, and the weak emission at around 435 nm arises from the COOH-related surface trap.

The XPS characterization gives consistent conclusion with respect to the infrared absorption study. Fig. 4b shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s. For both types of as-prepared DNCs, the strong peak at 284.1/283.7 eV is ascribed to the sp3-hybridized carbon atoms in diamond [28], and this confirms the diamond structure of the products. The post-treatments (N2H4·H2O-passivation and HCl-treatment) do not alter the crystal structure. For the G-DNCs, after passivation, the CO signals become much weaker, they are C*C(O)O at 285.3 eV [36], C(O)OC* at 286.8 eV [37], and C*O at 288.1 eV. On the other hand, the peak at 284.8 eV that is ascribed to C*N [38,39] arises. Fig. 4c shows the spectra of O 1s. After passivation of the G-DNCs, the C(O*)O signal at 531.6 eV diminishes and meanwhile, the CO*C signal (531.2 eV) becomes obvious. The presence of N 1s signals in the passivated G-DNCs and A-DNCs (Fig. 4d) demonstrates that nitrogen atoms are bonded to their surface. For the A-DNCs, the C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s spectra all indicate that after HCl-treatment, the C(O)NH2 signals become weak whereas the CO signals stand out. After HCl-treatment (Fig. 4b), the C*C(O)NH2 signal at 285.4eV [40] and C*(O)NH2 signal at 287.4 eV [41] nearly vanish, meanwhile, the signal of sp3-hybridized carbon atoms at 285.0 eV and the C*O signal at 288.0 eV stand out. For O 1s spectra (Fig. 4c), the strong peak at 530.7 eV that is assigned to C(O*)NH2 [42] decreases significantly, meanwhile, the C(O*)O signal at 531.6 eV becomes obvious. The spectra of N 1s (Fig. 4d) show that after HCl-treatment, the strong signal at 399.0 eV that is ascribed to C(O)N*H2 [42] diminishes considerably, while the signal from CN* at 400.2 eV [43] keeps strong. Therefore, the variation of the various XPS signals after surface treatments of the DNCs further confirm the above deduction on the origin of each emission band in the G-DNCs and A-DNCs.

Why are there four pairs of excitation/emission bands arising from the CO-related surface traps? The 410/527 nm excitation/emission band had also been observed in the C8 carbon NCs and was confirmed to arise from the C(O)O [ester group, abbreviated as C(O)O] related surface trap [30]. The identical behaviors suggest that these excitation/emission bands in both C8 carbon NCs and DNCs have the same origin. The 460/614 nm excitation/emission band should arise from the CCC(O)O-related surface trap because on the one hand, this pair of excitation and emission bands shows similar variation law with respect to the pair of 410/527 nm excitation/emission band after the DNCs experience various surface treatments; on the other hand, it is a common knowledge that conjugation of a CC bond to the ester group usually leads to red shift of light absorption by over 30–40 nm [44], which explains why there is an around 50-nm red shift for the 460-nm excitation band relative to the 410-nm excitation band. The 326/438 nm excitation/emission band had been observed in the 3CSiC NCs [45], which were identified to arise from the COOH-related surface defect. The currently observed excitation/emission band shares the same features with respect to that and hence they should have the same origin. The 310/385 nm excitation/emission band had also been observed in the 3CSiC NCs [46]. They also arise from the COOH-related surface defect. The difference between these two pairs of excitation/emission bands lies in that there are more methyl groups linked to the COOH-related surface functional group to yield the 320/433 nm excitation/emission band compared with the 310/390 nm excitation/emission band. This is consistent with the chemistry knowledge that addition of more methyl groups to the carboxylic acid group makes its light absorption shift to red by about 5 nm per methyl group [44]. This explains the small red shift of the 320-nm excitation band relative to the 310-nm excitation band. In practice, the existence of the fluorescent surface defects is also confirmed by measurement of the mass spectra of the samples, as shown in Fig. S2. The mass spectra confirm not only existence of the various chemical groups on the surface of the G-DNCs and A-DNCs but also their variations after passivation of the G-DNCs or HCl treatment of the A-DNCs. These variations are well consistent with that observed from the FTIR and XPS characterizations (Fig. 4).

The emission band of the A-DNCs shows reversible conversion after acid or alkali treatment. As shown in Fig. S3a, the PL of the A-DNCs is strong for pH > 6, while it drops rapidly when pH < 6. Under strong acid condition, the PL nearly quenches. Fig. S3b shows clearly the reversible variation of the emission band with varying pH. The as-prepared A-DNCs show strong PL at around 447 nm. After HCl treatment, the PL becomes very weak and meanwhile, the emission peak shifts from 447 nm to 435 nm. Surprisingly, after subsequent introduction of KOH, the strong 447-nm emission band recovers. This interesting phenomenon is caused by alternating processes of protonation and deprotonation. The C(O)NH2 reacts with H+ under strong acid condition, generating C(O)NH3 +, as a result, the PL centered at 447 nm arising from C(O)NH2 nearly quenches and the emission at around 435 nm arising from the carboxylic acid group becomes obvious. After subsequent introduction of KOH, the C(O)NH3 + releases H+ by deprotonation and thus the C(O)NH2-related strong PL recovers. This sensitive dependence of PL intensity and wavelength on pH suggests that the A-DNCs are ideal sensor for detecting variation of pH of the environment, especially pH of body fluids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical study of surface defects of diamond nanocrystals
ce:section.ce:para: To further clarify the roles of the various oxygen (nitrogen) surface defects in luminescence of the DNCs, we performed TDDFT calculations [47,48] considering various surface functional groups. We employed the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange-correlation functional [49] with the double-zeta plus polarization (DZP) basis set [50] implemented in the Amsterdam density-functional program package (ADF2013) [51]. The ground-state geometries of the DNCs were optimized at the DFT level, and with which the electronic excitation energies were calculated by using TDDFT. The optical absorption gap was computed as the difference between the energy levels of the ground state and the first excited singlet state. We constructed six types of DNCs with different surface functional groups including COOH, (CH3)COOH, C(O)NH2, COC, C(O)O, and CCC(O)O, since existence of such groups on the surfaces of the DNCs was confirmed by the infrared absorption and XPS studies as mentioned above. As shown in Fig. 5 , the isosurfaces of HOMO and LUMO in six types of DNCs are very different, indicating that the passivation with different functional groups leads to different surface states, which eventually induce distinct emission characteristics.

The TDDFT calculation shows that addition of the (CH3)COOH functional group on the surface of the diamond nanocrystal causes red shift of the absorption gap by about 0.05 eV relative to that of the COOH-terminated diamond nanocrystal. This explains the difference in the absorption peaks observed in the PLE spectra, that is, 310 nm for COOH-DNC and 320 nm for (CH3)COOH-DNC. We find that the HOMO of COOH-DNC or (CH3)COOH-DNC is highly delocalized, and the LUMO is strongly localized near the bonded surface functional group. In contrast, both HOMO and LUMO are localized around the surface functional group in C(O)NH2-DNCs, leading to a large spatial overlap between these two molecular orbitals. In practice, the transition between LUMO and HOMO contribute more than 90% to the first absorption peak of the diamond nanocrystal. According to the quantum theory [52], the spontaneous transition rate (R) of an electron between two quantum states of a system is
R ∝
|
∫
ψ 2 ∗
e
r ⇀
ψ 1
d V
|
2
, where
ψ 2
and
ψ 1
are respectively wave functions of the higher energy state and lower energy state, e is the electron charge, and
r ⇀
refers to the position vector of the electron. As a result, the electron transition rate in the C(O)NH2-DNCs is much larger than that in the COOH-DNCs or C(O)O-DNC. Indeed, our TDDFT calculation shows that the light absorption intensity of the C(O)NH2-DNCs is an order of magnitude larger than that of the COOH-DNCs and C(O)O-DNC. This result agrees well with the experimental result. The calculation also reveals that conjugation of a CC double bond to the C(O)O functional group strongly alters the HOMO and LUMO wave functions (Fig. 5) and the energy levels of the diamond nanocrystal. Although the electron negativity of oxygen is larger than that of carbon, the HOMO is more distributed on CC than on C(O)O. The conjugation of CC to the C(O)O group results in deeper surface states and remarkable red-shift of the optical absorption, being consistent with the experimental result. Note that the frontier orbital distribution of the COC functional group is different from those of other functional groups. This phenomenon may arise from the Jahn-Teller effect because the epoxy functional group results in significant structure distortion to diamond nanocrystal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy level distribution of surface defects
ce:section.ce:para: Base on the above deduction on the correspondence between the several pairs of excitation/emission bands and the identified surface traps as well as the knowledge on the energy level of the various surface traps, we schematically (Fig. 6 ) depict the complete scenario of energy level distribution of various surface traps within the forbidden gap between the conduction band and the valence band of the DNC. There are totally four types of surface states responsible for the observed multicolor luminescence of the DNCs. As mentioned above, the light absorptions/emissions at around 310/390 and 320/433 nm arise from the COOH-related surface defects; additional bonding of methyl groups in the adjacent of the COOH group alters the energy levels slightly. The light absorption/emission at 360/447 nm originates from the C(O)NH2-related surface defect. The light absorption/emission at around 368/470 nm stems from the COC-related surface defect. It should be noted that there are more oxygen bridge bonds on the surface of the diamond nanocrystal compared with other luminescent functional groups, hence the oxygen bridge bonds can form a cluster on the particle surface. As a result, the resultant luminescence varies in wavelength from one nanocrystal to another. Since the observed emission band is actually the superimposition of contributions from all the particles in an ensemble, therefore, the 470-nm emission band should have some shift in peak wavelength as the excitation wavelength varies. This explains the experimental phenomenon. Finally, the light absorptions/emissions at 410/527 and 460/614 nm arise from the C(O)O-related surface defects, and conjugation of a CC double bond to the C(O)O group on the particle surface causes considerable red shift of the light absorption and emission.

In practice, the currently identified various oxygen surface defects not only play an important role in luminescence of the DNCs, but also in other carbon-containing NCs. Fig. S4 summarizes all the known NCs that exhibit luminescence fully or partially arising from these oxygen surface defects. They include various carbon (graphite, diamond, C8), 3CSiC, and C3N4 NCs. The PL with an emission band at about 520 nm and an excitation band at about 416 nm arises frequently in the graphite NCs [35,53]. The C8-structured carbon NCs show double-band PL at 432 and 520 nm with corresponding excitation bands at 325 and 415 nm [30]. The blue light emissions at 385 and 438 nm with excitation bands at 310 and 326 nm had been observed in the 3CSiC NCs [45,46]. The light emission at around 526 nm was observed in the graphitic C3N4 NCs [54]. Therefore, the oxygen surface defects are popularly and intrinsically present in the carbon-containing NCs, and their roles in luminescence of these nanostructures are very important and should be taken into account as far as their optical properties are concerned.

In summary, we have investigated the underlying mechanism behind the peculiar luminescence behaviors of the DNCs both experimentally and theoretically. The luminescence spectroscopy and surface structure characterization along with first principles calculation together reveal the existence of various types of luminescent oxygen (nitrogen)-related surface defects in the DNCs. The calculation shows that the spatial overlap between LUMO and HOMO of the amide-terminated DNCs leads to very high radiative transition rate and quantum yield in contrast to relatively weak luminescence in oxygen-terminated DNCs. The identified various oxygen surface defects are popular and play important roles in luminescence of many other carbon-element-containing nanocrystals. These findings highly improve our understanding of the common luminescence mechanism of this whole family of nanocrystals.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China No. 11274063 and No. 11574047.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.07.067.
